1.pericope_label: Let Us Go to the House of the Lord
1.synthesis: Psalm 122 is the pilgrimage psalm of arrival, expressing the joy of the community at the prospect of entering Jerusalem and the house of YHWH. The opening is one of the great expressions of corporate liturgical joy: “I was glad when they said to me, ‘Let us go to the house of the Lord!’” (Ps 122:1). The arrival is narrated in present experience: “Our feet have been standing within your gates, O Jerusalem!” (122:2). The city is described with almost architectural affection: “Jerusalem—built as a city that is bound firmly together, to which the tribes go up, the tribes of the Lord, as was decreed for Israel, to give thanks to the name of the Lord” (122:3–4). The gathered tribes renew covenant loyalty through thanksgiving; the city is also the seat of justice: “There thrones for judgment were set, the thrones of the house of David” (122:5).
1.historical_context: Psalm 122 is a Davidic Song of Ascents, positioned at the pilgrim’s moment of arrival in Jerusalem after the journey begun in Psalm 120. The description of Jerusalem as “bound firmly together” (122:3) reflects the city’s architectural unity and its function as the central gathering point for all Israel’s tribes. The tribal pilgrimage was not merely religious but civic: the gathering of the twelve tribes at the central sanctuary re-enacted and renewed the covenant unity of the people. The thrones of the house of David (122:5) represent the judicial function of the Davidic monarchy as the seat of justice and the enforcer of Torah. Jesus references this geography in Matthew 5:35 when he calls Jerusalem “the city of the great King”—echoing the psalm’s understanding of Jerusalem as the royal center of divine governance.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 122 divides into two sections by its own content: the arrival and celebration of Jerusalem (vv. 1–5) and the prayer for Jerusalem’s peace (vv. 6–9), with the division at verse 6 following the paragraph structure. The first section moves from the announcement of gladness (v. 1) through the experience of arrival (v. 2) to the description of the city’s character—its unity, its pilgrim function, its judicial role (vv. 3–5). The accumulated description builds toward the prayer of the second section: the gladness of arriving motivates the intercessory concern for the city’s welfare. The pilgrim who has longed to come now prays that the city will remain what it was built to be.
1.theological_application: The gladness of arriving at the house of the Lord (122:1) models the posture of corporate worship: the gathering of God’s people is not merely convenient but joyful, the anticipated destination of a journey. Communities shaped by the first section of Psalm 122 recover the sense of the gathered assembly as an arrival—not a routine obligation but the fulfillment of a longing expressed in the movement of pilgrimage. The city that is “bound firmly together” (122:3) and to which the tribes go up suggests that the unity of God’s people is not merely social cohesion but has a center: the name of YHWH and the justice administered in his city. Worship and justice are both dimensions of what Jerusalem represents in this psalm—and both are reasons for the pilgrim’s joy.
1.christology: “I was glad when they said to me, ‘Let us go to the house of the Lord!’” (122:1) is fulfilled in Christ’s own journey to Jerusalem, particularly in the triumphal entry when the crowd welcomed him with Hosannas (Matt 21:9) as the son of David coming to his city. Christ who wept over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) was the ultimate lover of the city’s peace, and his coming was the arrival of the one whose throne the Davidic thrones (122:5) anticipated. The eschatological Jerusalem—the new Jerusalem coming down out of heaven from God (Rev 21:2)—is the fulfillment of what this psalm’s pilgrim arrives toward: the city perfectly and permanently inhabited by the divine presence, where every tribe gathers and every throne judges righteously.

6.synthesis: The second section of Psalm 122 turns from description of Jerusalem to intercession for it, with one of the most memorable commands in the pilgrimage collection: “Pray for the peace of Jerusalem! May they be secure who love you! Peace be within your walls and security within your towers!” (Ps 122:6–7). The motivation for the prayer is double—relational and theological: “For my brothers and companions’ sake I will say, ‘Peace be within you!’ For the sake of the house of the Lord our God, I will seek your good” (122:8–9). The peace prayed for is shalom—not merely the absence of war but the comprehensive wellbeing of a city living under YHWH’s governance. The Hebrew plays on the name Jerusalem (Yerushalayim) and the word for peace (shalom), so that praying for Jerusalem’s peace is etymologically built into the city’s very name.
6.historical_context: The command to “pray for the peace of Jerusalem” (122:6) has had a long life in both Jewish and Christian prayer traditions as a mandate to intercede for the holy city. The Hebrew wordplay between Yerushalayim (Jerusalem) and shalom (peace) runs through the entire section: “sha'alu shalom Yerushalayim”—ask for the peace of Jerusalem. The dual motivation in verses 8–9—for the sake of brothers and companions, and for the sake of YHWH’s house—reflects the Psalter’s integration of social and theological concern: the city’s peace matters because it is where human community and divine presence meet. The wall and tower imagery (122:7) is architectural security language, but its theological weight comes from the divine presence that makes the city secure beyond any human fortification.
6.literary_structure: The second section (vv. 6–9) is organized around two motivations for the peace-prayer, signaled by the repeated “for the sake of” (lema'an) in verses 8 and 9: love of brothers and companions, and love of YHWH’s house. The two motivations are not alternatives but complements—the human community gathered around divine worship is the context in which both can be named together. The peace wished upon the city’s walls and towers in verse 7 is then extended outward: what the pilgrim prays for the city is what the city does for its inhabitants—“they shall be secure who love you.” The security of the city is inseparable from the security of those who love it, and both rest on the divine presence that makes the house of YHWH central to Jerusalem’s identity.
6.theological_application: “For the sake of the house of the Lord our God, I will seek your good” (122:9) names a motivation for civic concern that is distinctive to a community shaped by YHWH’s presence: the good of the city is worth seeking because YHWH’s house is there. Communities shaped by this section of Psalm 122 learn that intercession for the wellbeing of shared places and communities is a form of worship, not a distraction from it—the prayer for Jerusalem’s peace is offered within the temple complex, by those who have traveled to YHWH’s house. The expansion from personal benefit (“my brothers and companions”) to theological ground (“the house of the Lord our God”) teaches that love of God and love of community are both legitimate motivations for intercession, and neither is sufficient without the other.
6.christology: The command to “pray for the peace of Jerusalem” (122:6) finds its christological depth in Christ’s own weeping over Jerusalem’s failure to receive its peace: “Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace! But now they are hidden from your eyes” (Luke 19:42). Christ prayed for Jerusalem’s peace and wept when it was rejected. The eschatological answer to Psalm 122’s prayer is the new Jerusalem where peace is finally permanent: “He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore” (Rev 21:4). The peace of Jerusalem prayed for in the psalm is the peace Christ brings—not as the world gives but as the Prince of Peace who is himself the city’s eternal shalom (Isa 9:6).
